1.notes:
Ephesians - Chapter 1
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Paul praises God for every spiritual blessing in Christ — election, predestination, redemption, and the sealing of the Holy Spirit as a guarantee of our inheritance — then prays that believers would know the surpassing greatness of God's power toward them.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Paul the Apostle (v.1). Written from prison in Rome (cf. 3:1, 4:1,
6:20), approximately AD 60-62. Ephesians is one of Paul's four "Prison
Epistles" (along with Philippians, Colossians, and Philemon).

Recipient: The church at Ephesus — a major city in Asia Minor (modern Turkey),
home to the temple of Artemis (one of the Seven Wonders). Paul spent over two
years there (Acts 19:10), and the church became a center for the gospel in
Asia. Some early manuscripts omit "at Ephesus" (v.1), suggesting this may
have been a circular letter to multiple churches.

Purpose: Unlike most of Paul's letters, Ephesians does not address a specific
problem. It is a theological masterpiece — a sweeping vision of God's eternal
purpose in Christ and the church. Chapters 1-3 are doctrinal (what God has
done); chapters 4-6 are practical (how we should live).

Chapter 1 Overview: One of the richest chapters in Scripture. Verses 3-14 are
a single sentence in Greek — a breathless doxology covering the work of the
Father (vv.3-6), the Son (vv.7-12), and the Spirit (vv.13-14). Verses 15-23
are Paul's prayer for the Ephesians to understand what they have in Christ.

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/ephesians-1/
  Guzik provides thorough analysis of the Trinitarian doxology (vv.3-14),
  the blessings "in Christ," election, redemption, sealing, and Paul's prayer.

Charles Spurgeon:
  Sermon: "All Spiritual Blessings" on v.3 (MTP Vol. 28, No. 1679)
  "ALL spiritual blessings. Not some — all. Not will bless — hath blessed.
  Past tense. Done. Finished. Every blessing you will ever need is already
  yours in Christ. You are not working toward blessing — you are working FROM
  blessing. The storehouse is full and the key is in your hand."
  Sermon: "Accepted in the Beloved" on v.6 (MTP Vol. 5, No. 286)
  "Accepted — not tolerated, not endured, not barely admitted — accepted.
  And not in yourself — in the Beloved. God sees you in Christ. When He looks
  at you, He sees His Son. You are as accepted as Christ is accepted. Let
  that sink into your soul and drive out every fear."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.3              | KJV         | "heavenly places"
v.3              | ESV         | Both equivalent. — Greek "epouraniois" — "the heavenlies." Used 5 times in Ephesians (1:3, 1:20, 2:6, 3:10, 6:12). A uniquely Ephesian concept — the spiritual realm where believers are already seated with Christ.
v.3              | NET         | "Blessed is the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, who has blessed us with every spiritual blessing in the heavenly realms in Christ" — "heavenly realms" (tois epouraniois = the heavenlies). WEB: "in the heavenly places." KJV: "in heavenly places." A key Ephesians phrase (1:3, 20; 2:6; 3:10; 6:12) — the spiritual dimension where believers are already seated.
v.4              | KJV         | "chosen us in him before the foundation of the world"
v.4              | ESV         | "chose us in him before the foundation of the world" — Greek "exelexato" (aorist middle) — God chose for Himself. The timing: before creation. The sphere: "in Christ." The purpose: holiness.
v.4              | NET         | "he chose us in Christ before the foundation of the world" — "before the foundation" (pro katabolEs kosmou). WEB: "before the foundation of the world." Election is pre-temporal — decided before creation, not in response to foreseen faith or merit.
v.6              | KJV         | "accepted in the beloved" — Greek "echaritōsen" — graced, favored, made objects of grace. Same root as "grace" (charis). ESV: "blessed us in the Beloved." NET: "freely bestowed on us in the One he loves."
v.7              | NET         | "In him we have redemption through his blood, the forgiveness of our trespasses" — "redemption" (apolytrOsin = ransom/release by payment). WEB: "redemption through his blood." KJV: "redemption through his blood." Slave-market language — purchased out of bondage at the cost of blood.
v.10             | KJV         | "dispensation of the fulness of times"
v.10             | ESV         | "as a plan for the fullness of time" — Greek "oikonomia" — administration, stewardship, plan. God's plan for the culmination of history: unite all things in Christ.
v.13             | KJV         | "sealed"
v.13             | ESV         | Both equivalent. — Greek "esphragisthēte" — stamped with a seal of ownership. In the ancient world, a seal indicated: (1) ownership, (2) authenticity, (3) security. The Spirit is God's seal on us.
v.13             | NET         | "you were sealed with the promised Holy Spirit" — "sealed" (esphragisthEte = stamped with a seal of ownership). WEB: "you were sealed with the Holy Spirit of promise." KJV: "ye were sealed with that holy Spirit of promise." A seal marks ownership, authenticity, and security.
v.14             | KJV         | "earnest"
v.14             | ESV         | "is the guarantee" — Greek "arrabōn" — down payment, deposit, guarantee. A commercial term: the first installment that guarantees the full payment. The Spirit now is the guarantee of glory later.
v.19             | KJV         | KJV uses four Greek words for power: "dunamis" (inherent power), "energeia" (working, active energy), "kratos" (manifested strength), "ischus" (might, force). Paul piles up power words to convey the immensity of God's power available to believers.
v.19             | NET         | "what is the incomparably great power for us who believe" — "incomparably great" (hyperballon megethos = surpassing greatness). WEB: "the exceeding greatness of his power toward us who believe." The same power that raised Christ from the dead (v.20) works in believers.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term             | Definition                                                   | Notes
-----------------|-------------------------------------------------------------|------
Heavenly places  | The spiritual realm where believers are already positioned in Christ. Not a future location but a present spiritual reality. | 
Election/Chosen  | God's sovereign choice of believers before creation. A doctrine of comfort: your salvation originates in God's eternal purpose, not your fragile decision. | 
Predestination   | God's predetermined plan for those He chose — specifically, adoption as sons (v.5) and conformity to Christ (Rom 8:29). | 
Redemption       | Greek "apolutrōsis" — release by payment of a ransom. The price: Christ's blood. The result: forgiveness of sins. | 
Sealed           | Marked with God's seal of ownership through the Holy Spirit. Indicates ownership, authenticity, and security. | 
Earnest/Deposit  | The Holy Spirit as God's down payment guaranteeing our full inheritance. What we have now is a foretaste of what's coming. | 
Mystery          | Greek "mustērion" — not something mysterious but something previously hidden now revealed. God's plan to unite all things in Christ. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "All spiritual blessings" (v.3). You already have EVERY spiritual blessing
   in Christ. You're not working toward God's favor — you already have it.
   How does this change the way you approach today?

2. "Chosen before the foundation of the world" (v.4). God chose you before
   you existed. Your salvation doesn't depend on your performance — it
   originates in God's eternal purpose. Does this give you security or raise
   questions? Sit with both.

3. "Accepted in the beloved" (v.6). You are accepted — not because of what
   you've done but because of who you're in. Where do you still feel
   unaccepted? Can you receive this truth?

4. "Sealed with the Holy Spirit" (v.13). God has put His seal of ownership on
   you. You belong to Him. You are secure. How does this affect your sense of
   identity and security?

5. Paul prays for "the spirit of wisdom and revelation" (v.17). What would
   change if you truly knew the hope of God's calling, the riches of His
   inheritance, and the greatness of His power? Pray this prayer for yourself.

2.ESV:
1. And you were dead in the trespasses and sins
2. in which you once walked, following the course of this world, following the prince of the power of the air, the spirit that is now at work in the sons of disobedience—
3. among whom we all once lived in the passions of our flesh, carrying out the desires of the flesh and the mind, and were by nature children of wrath, like the rest of mankind.
4. But God, being rich in mercy, because of the great love with which he loved us,
5. even when we were dead in our trespasses, made us alive together with Christ—by grace you have been saved—
6. and raised us up with him and seated us with him in the heavenly places in Christ Jesus,
7. so that in the coming ages he might show the immeasurable riches of his grace in kindness toward us in Christ Jesus.
8. For by grace you have been saved through faith. And this is not your own doing; it is the gift of God,
9. not a result of works, so that no one may boast.
10. For we are his workmanship, created in Christ Jesus for good works, which God prepared beforehand, that we should walk in them.
11. Therefore remember that at one time you Gentiles in the flesh, called “the uncircumcision” by what is called the circumcision, which is made in the flesh by hands—
12. remember that you were at that time separated from Christ, alienated from the commonwealth of Israel and strangers to the covenants of promise, having no hope and without God in the world.
13. But now in Christ Jesus you who once were far off have been brought near by the blood of Christ.
14. For he himself is our peace, who has made us both one and has broken down in his flesh the dividing wall of hostility
15. by abolishing the law of commandments expressed in ordinances, that he might create in himself one new man in place of the two, so making peace,
16. and might reconcile us both to God in one body through the cross, thereby killing the hostility.
17. And he came and preached peace to you who were far off and peace to those who were near.
18. For through him we both have access in one Spirit to the Father.
19. So then you are no longer strangers and aliens, but you are fellow citizens with the saints and members of the household of God,
20. built on the foundation of the apostles and prophets, Christ Jesus himself being the cornerstone,
21. in whom the whole structure, being joined together, grows into a holy temple in the Lord.
22. In him you also are being built together into a dwelling place for God by the Spirit.

2.notes:
Ephesians - Chapter 2
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
"By grace are ye saved through faith; and that not of yourselves: it is the gift of God" — once dead in sin but made alive with Christ, Jew and Gentile are reconciled into one new humanity through the cross, built together into God's dwelling place.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Paul the Apostle (see Chapter 1 notes for full authorship details).

Historical Context: Chapter 2 is one of the greatest summaries of the gospel
ever written. It moves from humanity's hopeless condition (vv.1-3) to God's
gracious salvation (vv.4-10) to the unity of Jew and Gentile in Christ
(vv.11-22). The famous "But God" of verse 4 is the hinge of human history.

Dead in Sin (vv.1-3): Before Christ, all people — Jew and Gentile alike —
were spiritually dead, controlled by the world, the devil ("the prince of the
power of the air"), and the flesh. "By nature the children of wrath" (v.3) —
not just sinners by choice but by nature.

But God (vv.4-10): The two greatest words in Scripture. Despite our deadness,
God — rich in mercy, great in love — made us alive, raised us up, and seated
us with Christ in heavenly places. Salvation is "by grace through faith" (v.8)
— not of works. Yet we are "created in Christ Jesus unto good works" (v.10).
Grace saves; works follow.

One New Man (vv.11-22): Gentiles were once outsiders — "without Christ,"
"aliens," "strangers," "having no hope" (v.12). But Christ broke down the
dividing wall between Jew and Gentile, creating "one new man" (v.15). Both
now have access to the Father by one Spirit (v.18). Together they form God's
temple (vv.20-22).

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/ephesians-2/
  Guzik provides detailed commentary on spiritual death, "But God," salvation
  by grace, God's workmanship, and the unity of Jew and Gentile in Christ.

Charles Spurgeon:
  Sermon: "But God" on v.4 (MTP Vol. 25, No. 1476)
  "But God. Two words that change everything. You were dead — but God made
  you alive. You were condemned — but God justified you. You were far off —
  but God brought you near. Every testimony in heaven will begin with these
  two words: 'I was lost — but God.' Never forget where you were, and never
  forget who rescued you."
  Sermon: "By Grace Are Ye Saved" on v.8 (MTP Vol. 17, No. 1027)
  "By grace — not by works, not by merit, not by effort, not by ceremony.
  Through faith — the empty hand that receives the gift. And that not of
  yourselves — even the faith is God's gift. From first to last, salvation
  is of the Lord. Boasting is excluded. Glory belongs to God alone."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "quickened" — made alive. Not in the Greek text but implied from
v.1              | NET         | "And although you were dead in your transgressions and sins" — "dead" (nekrous = corpses). WEB: "you were dead in your transgressions and sins." Not sick or weak — DEAD. Dead people cannot contribute to their own resurrection.
v.2              | KJV         | "prince of the power of the air"
v.2              | ESV         | Both equivalent. — Satan described as ruler of the atmospheric/spiritual realm. "Air" (Greek "aēr") — the lower atmosphere, the realm between heaven and earth where evil spirits operate.
v.3              | KJV         | "conversation" — Greek "anastrophē" — conduct, way of life (not speech). ESV: "lived." Our entire manner of life was in the flesh.
v.3              | KJV         | "children of wrath"
v.3              | ESV         | Both equivalent. — Greek "tekna phusēi orgēs" — by nature objects of wrath. Not just sinners by choice but by birth — the doctrine of original sin.
v.4              | NET         | "But God, being rich in mercy, because of his great love with which he loved us" — "But God" (ho de theos) — the great turning point. WEB: "But God, being rich in mercy." KJV: "But God, who is rich in mercy." Two words that change everything — divine intervention into human helplessness.
v.8              | KJV         | "and that not of yourselves"
v.8              | ESV         | "faith. And this is not your own doing" — What is "that"? Grammatically, the neuter "touto" doesn't match "faith" (feminine) or "grace" (feminine). It refers to the entire salvation package — the whole process of being saved by grace through faith is God's gift.
v.8              | NET         | "For by grace you are saved through faith, and this is not from yourselves, it is the gift of God" — "this" (touto) — what is the gift? Grace? Faith? The whole package? The neuter pronoun doesn't match "faith" (feminine) or "grace" (feminine) — it refers to the entire salvation arrangement. WEB: "it is the gift of God."
v.10             | KJV         | "workmanship"
v.10             | ESV         | Both equivalent. — Greek "poiēma" — a work of art, a masterpiece. Root of English "poem." You are God's poem, His masterpiece, His creative work.
v.10             | NET         | "For we are his creative work, having been created in Christ Jesus for good works" — "creative work" (poiEma = masterpiece/workmanship). WEB: "we are his workmanship." KJV: "we are his workmanship." English "poem" derives from this — we are God's artistic creation.
v.14             | KJV         | "middle wall of partition"
v.14             | ESV         | "down in his flesh the dividing wall" — Likely refers to the literal wall in the Jerusalem temple that separated the Court of the Gentiles from the inner courts. Signs warned Gentiles that crossing meant death. Christ demolished that wall.
v.14             | NET         | "For he is our peace, the one who made both groups into one and who destroyed the middle wall of partition" — "middle wall of partition" (to mesotoichon tou phragmou). WEB: "the middle wall of separation." KJV: "the middle wall of partition." Likely refers to the temple barrier separating Gentile and Jewish courts.
v.15             | KJV         | "one new man"
v.15             | ESV         | Both equivalent. — Greek "kainos anthrōpos" — not a patched-up version of Jew or Gentile but an entirely new humanity. The church is not Gentiles becoming Jews or Jews becoming Gentiles — it's both becoming something new.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term                           | Definition                                                   | Notes
-------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------|------
Quickened                      | Made alive. Spiritual resurrection from the dead. God's act of giving life to those who were spiritually dead. | 
Prince of the power of the air | Satan, the ruler of the spiritual realm between heaven and earth, who influences the world system. | 
Children of wrath              | Humanity's natural condition: objects of God's righteous anger due to inherited sinful nature (original sin). | 
But God                        | The turning point of the gospel. Despite human hopelessness, God intervened with mercy and love. | 
Workmanship                    | Greek "poiēma" — a masterpiece, a work of art. Believers are God's creative masterpiece, designed for good works. | 
Middle wall of partition       | The barrier between Jew and Gentile, symbolized by the temple wall. Christ destroyed it through the cross. | 
One new man                    | The church as a new humanity — neither Jew nor Gentile but a new creation in Christ where both are united. | 
Chief corner stone             | Christ as the foundation stone that holds the entire building together and determines its alignment. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "Dead in trespasses and sins" (v.1). Before Christ, you weren't sick —
   you were dead. Dead people can't help themselves. How does understanding
   your former deadness increase your gratitude for God's grace?

2. "But God" (v.4). What's your "But God" story? Where were you headed, and
   how did God intervene? Can you trace His mercy in your life?

3. "By grace are ye saved through faith" (v.8). Are you resting in grace or
   still trying to earn God's favor? Where does performance-based religion
   creep into your thinking?

4. "His workmanship" (v.10). You are God's masterpiece — created for specific
   good works He prepared in advance. What good works has God prepared for
   you? Are you walking in them?

5. "Made nigh by the blood of Christ" (v.13). If you were once "far off" —
   outside, alienated, hopeless — how does it feel to be "made nigh"? Do you
   live like an insider or still feel like a stranger?

6. "Built upon the foundation" (vv.20-22). You are part of God's living
   temple. How does being "builded together" with other believers shape your
   view of church and community?

3.notes:
Ephesians - Chapter 3
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Paul reveals the mystery hidden for ages — that Gentiles are fellow heirs with Israel in Christ — and prays that believers would be strengthened by the Spirit to comprehend the love of Christ which surpasses knowledge, being filled with all the fullness of God.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Paul the Apostle (see Chapter 1 notes for full authorship details).

Historical Context: Chapter 3 reveals the "mystery" that was hidden for ages
but now revealed: Gentiles are fellow heirs with Jews in Christ (v.6). Paul
sees himself as the specific steward of this mystery to the Gentiles (v.8).
The chapter concludes with one of the most magnificent prayers in Scripture
(vv.14-21) and a doxology that soars beyond human comprehension (v.20).

Paul's Digression (vv.1-13): Paul begins "For this cause I Paul..." (v.1)
but doesn't complete the thought until v.14. In between, he explains his
unique role as apostle to the Gentiles and the mystery revealed to him. He
is "the prisoner of Jesus Christ for you Gentiles" (v.1) — his imprisonment
is FOR them, not despite them.

The Mystery Revealed (vv.2-6): The mystery hidden for ages: "That the
Gentiles should be fellowheirs, and of the same body, and partakers of his
promise in Christ by the gospel" (v.6). Three "fellow" words: fellow-heirs,
fellow-members, fellow-partakers. Complete equality.

Paul's Ministry (vv.7-13): Paul — "less than the least of all saints" (v.8)
— was given grace to preach "the unsearchable riches of Christ" to Gentiles.
The church now displays God's "manifold wisdom" to spiritual powers (v.10).

Paul's Prayer (vv.14-19): Paul prays for: (1) inner strengthening by the
Spirit (v.16), (2) Christ dwelling in their hearts (v.17), (3) comprehension
of Christ's love in all its dimensions (v.18), (4) being filled with all the
fullness of God (v.19).

The Doxology (vv.20-21): "Exceeding abundantly above all that we ask or
think" — God's ability surpasses our imagination.

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/ephesians-3/
  Guzik covers Paul's imprisonment, the mystery of Gentile inclusion, the
  church's cosmic role, Paul's prayer, and the doxology.

Charles Spurgeon:
  Sermon: "Exceeding Abundantly" on v.20 (MTP Vol. 25, No. 1503)
  "Above all we ask — that would be enough. Above all we think — that goes
  further still. Abundantly above — more still. Exceeding abundantly above —
  Paul runs out of words. He piles up language because God's ability exceeds
  all human expression. Whatever you can imagine God doing for you — He can
  do infinitely more. Your imagination is the limit of your asking, but it
  is not the limit of His giving."
  Sermon: "The Love of Christ Which Passeth Knowledge" on v.19 (MTP Vol. 18)
  "To know the love which passeth knowledge — a paradox. You can know it
  experientially though you can never exhaust it intellectually. It is like
  standing at the ocean's edge — you can wade in, swim, even dive deep, but
  you will never reach the bottom or the far shore. His love has breadth
  enough for all, length enough for eternity, depth enough for the worst
  sinner, height enough to reach the throne."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "the prisoner of Jesus Christ"
v.1              | ESV         | "Paul, a prisoner of Christ Jesus on behalf" — Not Rome's prisoner but Christ's. Paul reframes his imprisonment: he is in chains because of his ministry to Gentiles, and he belongs to Christ, not Caesar.
v.5              | NET         | "that the Gentiles are fellow heirs, fellow members of the body, and fellow partakers of the promise in Christ Jesus" — three "fellow" (syn-) compounds: synklEronoma, syssOma, symmetocha. WEB: "fellow heirs and fellow members of the body, and fellow partakers of his promise." The triple syn- emphasizes full equality.
v.6              | Multiple    | Three "syn-" (together) compounds in Greek: "synklēronoma" (fellow- heirs), "syssōma" (fellow-body), "symmetocha" (fellow-partakers). Paul coins "syssōma" — it appears nowhere else in Greek literature. Complete, equal participation.
v.8              | KJV         | "less than the least"
v.8              | ESV         | "though I am the very least of all" — Greek "elachistoterō" — a comparative form of a superlative. Paul invents a word: "leaster than the least." Grammatically impossible but theologically profound — his humility grows as his understanding of grace deepens.
v.8              | NET         | "To me — Loss less than the least of all the saints — this grace was given" — "less than the least" (tO elachistoterO = a comparative of a superlative — grammatically impossible, emotionally powerful). WEB: "to me, the very least of all saints." Paul invents a word to express his unworthiness.
v.10             | KJV         | "manifold wisdom"
v.10             | ESV         | Both equivalent. — Greek "polupoikilos" — many-colored, multi-faceted, infinitely varied. Like a diamond with countless facets. The church displays God's wisdom in all its variety to spiritual powers.
v.16             | KJV         | "inner man"
v.16             | ESV         | "in your inner being" — Greek "esō anthrōpon" — the inner person, the spiritual self as opposed to the outer, physical self. The real you that needs strengthening.
v.16             | NET         | "that he may grant you to be strengthened with power through his Spirit in the inner person" — "inner person" (eis ton esO anthrOpon). WEB: "strengthened with power through his Spirit in the inner person." KJV: "strengthened with might by his Spirit in the inner man." The Spirit's primary work is internal.
v.18             | KJV         | "breadth, and length, and depth, and height"
v.18             | ESV         | "what is the breadth and length and height and depth" — Four dimensions of Christ's love. Some see: breadth = extends to all people; length = lasts forever; depth = reaches the lowest sinner; height = lifts to the highest glory.
v.18             | NET         | "may be able to comprehend with all the saints what is the breadth and length and height and depth" — four dimensions of Christ's love. WEB: "the breadth and length and height and depth." No object specified — the love is so vast it requires spatial metaphor yet exceeds all dimensions.
v.19             | KJV         | "filled with all the fulness of God"
v.19             | ESV         | "may be filled with all the fullness of God" — Greek "plērōma" — the same word used of Christ in Col 2:9. Believers are to be filled with the very fullness of God Himself — an inexhaustible goal.
v.20             | KJV         | "exceeding abundantly above"
v.20             | ESV         | "to do far more abundantly than all" — Greek "huper ek perissou" — super-abundantly beyond. Three intensifiers stacked: above + out of + abundance. Paul strains language to express God's limitless ability.
v.20             | NET         | "Now to him who by the power that is working within us is able to do far beyond all that we ask or think" — "far beyond" (hyper ek perissou = super-abundantly above). WEB: "exceedingly abundantly above all that we ask or think." KJV: "exceeding abundantly above all that we ask or think." Triple intensification — God's ability exceeds imagination.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term                | Definition                                                   | Notes
--------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------|------
Mystery             | Something previously hidden in God's plan, now revealed through the apostles. Specifically: Gentile equality in Christ. | 
Dispensation        | Greek "oikonomia" — stewardship, administration. Paul was given the stewardship of revealing God's grace to Gentiles. | 
Unsearchable riches | Greek "anexichniaston" — untraceable, unfathomable. Christ's riches cannot be fully explored or exhausted. | 
Manifold wisdom     | Greek "polupoikilos" — many-colored, multi-faceted. God's wisdom displayed in infinite variety through the church. | 
Inner man           | The spiritual self, the true person within. The seat of spiritual life that needs strengthening by the Spirit. | 
Rooted and grounded | Two metaphors: a tree with deep roots (stability) and a building with a solid foundation (security). Both in love. | 
Fullness of God     | The complete character and presence of God filling the believer. An inexhaustible, ever-expanding reality. | 
Doxology            | A formal expression of praise to God. Ephesians 3:20-21 is one of the greatest doxologies in Scripture. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "Less than the least of all saints" (v.8). Paul's humility grew as his
   understanding of grace deepened. Is that true for you? Does knowing more
   of God's grace make you more humble or more proud?

2. "The unsearchable riches of Christ" (v.8). Christ's riches are
   inexhaustible — you will never reach the bottom. What "riches of Christ"
   have you discovered recently? What remains unexplored?

3. "The manifold wisdom of God" (v.10). The church — with all its messiness —
   displays God's wisdom to spiritual powers. How does this change your view
   of the church? Of your role in it?

4. "Strengthened with might by his Spirit in the inner man" (v.16). Where do
   you need inner strengthening right now? Are you cultivating your inner life
   or neglecting it for external activity?

5. "To know the love of Christ, which passeth knowledge" (v.19). Have you
   experienced Christ's love in a way that goes beyond intellectual
   understanding? What would it look like to be "filled with all the fulness
   of God"?

6. "Exceeding abundantly above all that we ask or think" (v.20). What are you
   asking God for? Whatever it is — He can do infinitely more. Are your
   prayers too small?

4.notes:
Ephesians - Chapter 4
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Paul urges believers to walk worthy of their calling in unity, explains that Christ gave gifted leaders to equip the saints for ministry until all reach maturity, and calls them to put off the old self and put on the new self created in righteousness.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Paul the Apostle (see Chapter 1 notes for full authorship details).

Historical Context: Chapter 4 marks the transition from doctrine (chs.1-3) to
practice (chs.4-6). Paul's "therefore" (v.1) connects everything that follows
to everything that preceded. Because of who you are in Christ (chs.1-3), here
is how you should live (chs.4-6). The chapter covers three themes: unity in
the body (vv.1-16), putting off the old self (vv.17-24), and practical
holiness (vv.25-32).

Walk Worthy — Unity (vv.1-16): Paul urges believers to "walk worthy" of their
calling with humility, gentleness, patience, and love (vv.1-3). He lists seven
"ones" that form the basis of Christian unity (vv.4-6). Christ gave gifted
leaders — apostles, prophets, evangelists, pastors, teachers — to equip the
saints for ministry and build up the body (vv.11-12). The goal: maturity,
measured by "the stature of the fulness of Christ" (v.13).

Put Off the Old, Put On the New (vv.17-24): Gentile believers must no longer
live like pagans — darkened in understanding, alienated from God (vv.17-19).
They must "put off" the old man and "put on" the new man, created in
righteousness and holiness (vv.22-24). Like changing clothes.

Practical Holiness (vv.25-32): Specific commands: speak truth, handle anger
properly, don't steal but work and give, speak words that build up, don't
grieve the Spirit, put away bitterness, be kind and forgiving (vv.25-32).

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/ephesians-4/
  Guzik covers walking worthy, the seven "ones," spiritual gifts, the
  old/new man, and practical holiness commands.

Charles Spurgeon:
  Sermon: "Grieving the Holy Spirit" on v.30 (MTP Vol. 4, No. 182)
  "Grieve not the Holy Spirit. Not 'anger' — grieve. The word implies
  tenderness, love, sensitivity. A master is angered by a servant's
  disobedience. A father is grieved by a child's rebellion. The Spirit
  dwells in you as a loving person — and your sin causes Him sorrow. What
  grieves Him? Bitterness, wrath, anger, evil speaking — the sins of
  relationship. He is the Spirit of unity, and disunity grieves Him."
  Sermon: "The Measure of the Gift of Christ" on v.7 (MTP Vol. 32, No. 1908)
  "Grace according to the measure of the gift of Christ — not according to
  your merit, not according to your capacity, but according to Christ's gift.
  And what is the measure of Christ's gift? Immeasurable. He gives without
  limit because He is without limit."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "vocation" — Greek "klēsis" — calling. Not occupation but divine calling. ESV: "calling to which you have been called."
v.3              | NET         | "making every effort to keep the unity of the Spirit in the bond of peace" — "making every effort" (spoudazontes = being diligent/eager). WEB: "being eager to keep the unity of the Spirit." KJV: "Endeavouring to keep the unity." Unity exists; our job is to MAINTAIN it, not create it.
v.8              | KJV         | "led captivity captive"
v.8              | ESV         | "he led a host of captives, and" — Quotes Psalm 68:18 but changes "received gifts" to "gave gifts." Paul interprets Christ's ascension: He conquered His enemies (led captivity captive) and distributed the spoils (gave gifts to men).
v.9              | KJV         | "lower parts of the earth"
v.9              | ESV         | "into the lower regions, the earth?" — Debated. Options: (1) Christ's descent into Hades between death and resurrection, (2) the Incarnation (descending from heaven to earth), (3) Christ's burial. Most likely refers to the Incarnation or burial.
v.11             | KJV         | "pastors and teachers"
v.11             | ESV         | "the shepherds and teachers" — Greek grammar links these with one article ("tous de poimenas kai didaskalous"), suggesting one office: pastor-teachers. Those who shepherd by teaching.
v.11             | NET         | "It was he who gave some as apostles, some as prophets, some as evangelists, and some as pastors and teachers" — "gave" (edOken = gifted). WEB: "He gave some to be apostles." The leaders ARE the gifts to the church — not just their abilities but their persons.
v.12             | KJV         | KJV punctuation is crucial. Older versions: "For the perfecting of the saints, for the work of the ministry, for the edifying of the body." Better: "For the perfecting of the saints FOR the work of the ministry FOR the edifying of the body." Leaders equip saints; saints do ministry; the body is built up.
v.13             | NET         | "until we all attain to the unity of the faith and of the knowledge of the Son of God — to a mature person" — "mature person" (andra teleion = complete/full-grown man). WEB: "to a full grown man." KJV: "unto a perfect man." Not individual perfection but corporate maturity — the whole body growing up together.
v.15             | NET         | "speaking the truth in love, we will in all things grow up into him who is the head, that is, Christ" — "speaking the truth in love" (alEtheuontes en agapE = truthing in love — a rare verb). WEB: "speaking truth in love." Truth without love wounds; love without truth deceives. Both together produce growth.
v.22             | KJV         | "conversation" — Greek "anastrophē" — manner of life, conduct (not speech). ESV: "former manner of life."
v.26             | KJV         | "Be ye angry, and sin not"
v.26             | ESV         | "Be angry and do not sin" — Quotes Psalm 4:4. Anger itself is not sin — it's a God-given emotion. But it must be handled quickly ("let not the sun go down") and not given to the devil (v.27).
v.26             | NET         | "Be angry and do not sin; do not let the sun go down on the cause of your anger" — quoting Psalm 4:4. "Be angry" (orgizesthe) — anger itself is not sin; nursing it overnight is. WEB: "Be angry, and don't sin." KJV: "Be ye angry, and sin not." A time limit on anger — sunset is the deadline.
v.30             | KJV         | "grieve"
v.30             | ESV         | Both equivalent. — Greek "lupeō" — to cause sorrow, to sadden. The Spirit is a person who can be grieved — not an impersonal force.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term                                               | Definition                                                   | Notes
---------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------|------
Walk worthy                                        | Living in a manner consistent with one's calling in Christ. Conduct that matches identity. | 
Unity of the Spirit                                | The oneness already created by the Spirit among believers. Not something to create but to maintain. | 
Apostles, prophets, evangelists, pastors, teachers | The five gifted leadership roles Christ gave to equip the church for ministry. | 
Perfecting/Equipping                               | Greek "katartismos" — to mend, complete, prepare, train. Leaders prepare saints for their ministry work. | 
Old man/New man                                    | The former self (corrupted by sin) and the new self (created by God in righteousness). Put off/put on like clothes. | 
Grieve the Spirit                                  | To cause sorrow to the Holy Spirit through sin — especially relational sins like bitterness, anger, and unforgiveness. | 
Corrupt communication                              | Greek "sapros logos" — rotten words. Speech that tears down rather than builds up. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "Walk worthy" (v.1). Is your daily conduct consistent with your calling in
   Christ? Where is the gap between who you are positionally and how you live
   practically?

2. "Endeavouring to keep the unity" (v.3). Unity requires effort — it doesn't
   happen automatically. Where are you actively working to maintain unity in
   your relationships, your church, your community?

3. "For the perfecting of the saints, for the work of the ministry" (v.12).
   Every believer is called to ministry — not just pastors. What is YOUR
   ministry? How are you being equipped for it?

4. "Put off the old man... put on the new man" (vv.22-24). What specific
   "old man" habits do you need to take off? What "new man" qualities do you
   need to put on? Be specific.

5. "Be ye angry, and sin not" (v.26). How do you handle anger? Do you stuff
   it (letting the sun go down on it) or explode (sinning in it)? What does
   righteous anger look like?

6. "Be ye kind one to another, tenderhearted, forgiving one another" (v.32).
   Is there someone you need to forgive? The standard: "as God for Christ's
   sake hath forgiven you." How much has God forgiven you?

5.notes:
Ephesians - Chapter 5
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Believers are to walk as children of light, avoid all impurity, be filled with the Spirit rather than wine, and submit to one another — with marriage revealed as a profound mystery picturing Christ's sacrificial love for the church.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Paul the Apostle (see Chapter 1 notes for full authorship details).

Historical Context: Chapter 5 continues the practical section of Ephesians.
Paul moves from general holiness (4:25-32) to specific areas: walking in love
(vv.1-7), walking in light (vv.8-14), walking in wisdom (vv.15-21), and the
marriage relationship as a picture of Christ and the church (vv.22-33). The
marriage passage is one of the most significant in all of Scripture for
understanding both human marriage and Christ's love for His church.

Walk in Love (vv.1-7): Imitate God as beloved children. Walk in love as
Christ loved us and gave Himself for us (v.2). Sexual immorality, impurity,
and covetousness should not even be named among saints (v.3). These exclude
from God's kingdom (v.5).

Walk in Light (vv.8-14): "Ye were sometimes darkness, but now are ye light"
(v.8) — not just "in darkness" but darkness itself. Now light itself. Light
exposes darkness (v.13). "Awake thou that sleepest" (v.14) — a call to
spiritual alertness.

Walk in Wisdom (vv.15-21): Redeem the time (v.16). Understand God's will
(v.17). Be filled with the Spirit (v.18) — the key command of the passage.
The evidence of Spirit-filling: singing, thanksgiving, mutual submission
(vv.19-21).

Marriage: Christ and the Church (vv.22-33): Wives submit to husbands as the
church submits to Christ (vv.22-24). Husbands love wives as Christ loved the
church — sacrificially, sanctifyingly, nourishingly (vv.25-29). Marriage is a
"great mystery" pointing to Christ and the church (v.32).

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/ephesians-5/
  Guzik covers walking in love, light, and wisdom, being filled with the
  Spirit, and the marriage relationship as a picture of Christ and the church.

Charles Spurgeon:
  Sermon: "Be Filled with the Spirit" on v.18 (MTP Vol. 31, No. 1838)
  "Be not drunk with wine — that is the world's filling, the flesh's
  counterfeit. But be filled with the Spirit — that is God's filling, the
  true intoxication of the soul. Wine controls a man from without; the Spirit
  controls from within. Wine degrades; the Spirit elevates. Wine produces
  excess; the Spirit produces fruit. The command is present tense — keep on
  being filled. It is not a one-time experience but a continuous reality."
  Sermon: "Christ's Love to His Church" on v.25 (MTP Vol. 7, No. 404)
  "Husbands, love your wives as Christ loved the church. What a standard!
  He loved her when she was unlovely. He gave Himself for her — not His
  money, not His time, not His energy — Himself. He loved her to make her
  holy, not because she was holy. Husband, your love is to be sacrificial,
  sanctifying, and unconditional — like Christ's."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "followers"
v.1              | ESV         | "be imitators of God, as" — Greek "mimētai" — imitators. Root of English "mimic." Not just following but actively imitating God's character.
v.2              | NET         | "and live in love, just as Christ also loved us and gave himself for us, a sacrificial and fragrant offering to God" — "fragrant offering" (prosphoran kai thysian tO theO eis osmEn euOdias). WEB: "a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God." KJV: "a sweetsmelling savour." Christ's death pleased the Father — a voluntary, accepted sacrifice.
v.4              | KJV         | "jesting" — Greek "eutrapelia" — coarse joking, crude humor. Not all humor but specifically sexual innuendo and crude speech. ESV: "crude joking."
v.8              | KJV         | "ye were sometimes darkness"
v.8              | ESV         | "for at one time you were darkness, but now" — Not "in darkness" but darkness itself. Now "ye are light" — not "in the light" but light itself. Total identity change.
v.14             | KJV         | "Awake thou that sleepest"
v.14             | ESV         | "becomes visible is light. Therefore it says" — Possibly an early Christian hymn or baptismal formula. Not a direct OT quote but echoes Isaiah 60:1.
v.14             | NET         | "Awake, O sleeper! Rise from the dead, and Christ will shine on you!" — possibly an early Christian hymn or baptismal formula. WEB: "Awake, you who sleep, and arise from the dead, and Christ will shine on you." KJV: "Awake thou that sleepest." Source unknown — perhaps adapted from Isaiah 60:1.
v.16             | KJV         | "redeeming the time"
v.16             | ESV         | "making the best use of the time" — Greek "exagorazomenoi ton kairon" — buying up the opportunity. "Kairos" = opportune time, not "chronos" (clock time). Making the most of every opportunity.
v.18             | KJV         | "be filled"
v.18             | ESV         | Both equivalent. — Greek "plērousthe" — present passive imperative. Present = continuous (keep being filled). Passive = let God fill you. Imperative = it's a command, not a suggestion.
v.18             | NET         | "And do not get drunk with wine, which is debauchery, but be filled by the Spirit" — "be filled" (plErousthe = present passive imperative — keep on being filled). WEB: "be filled with the Spirit." KJV: "be filled with the Spirit." Not a one-time event but a continuous state — the ongoing alternative to drunkenness.
v.21             | KJV         | "submitting yourselves one to another"
v.21             | ESV         | "submitting to one another out of reverence for Christ" — This verse is the heading for the entire household code (5:22-6:9). Mutual submission is the overarching principle; specific roles follow.
v.25             | KJV         | "gave himself for it"
v.25             | ESV         | "and gave himself up for her" — Greek "paredōken heauton" — handed Himself over. The same word used of Judas betraying Jesus. Christ voluntarily handed Himself over to death for His bride.
v.25             | NET         | "Husbands, love your wives just as Christ loved the church and gave himself for her" — "gave himself" (heauton paredOken) — self-sacrificial love as the husband's model. WEB: "love your wives, even as Christ also loved the assembly." The standard is the cross — not feelings but sacrifice.
v.31             | KJV         | "joined"
v.31             | ESV         | "hold fast to his wife" — Greek "proskollēthēsetai" — glued to, cemented to. Permanent, inseparable union.
v.32             | KJV         | "mystery"
v.32             | ESV         | Both equivalent. — Greek "mustērion" — not something mysterious but something previously hidden now revealed. Marriage was always meant to picture Christ and the church — now that meaning is unveiled.
v.32             | NET         | "This mystery is great — but I am actually speaking with reference to Christ and the church" — "mystery" (mystErion) — marriage was always meant to picture something bigger. WEB: "This mystery is great, but I speak concerning Christ and the assembly." Marriage as icon of redemption.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term                   | Definition                                                   | Notes
-----------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------|------
Walk in love           | Living with sacrificial, Christ-like love as the governing principle of all relationships and decisions. | 
Children of light      | Believers whose identity is now light itself. They expose darkness and produce "goodness, righteousness, and truth" (v.9). | 
Redeeming the time     | Buying up every opportunity. Making the most of the limited time available because the days are evil. | 
Filled with the Spirit | The continuous, ongoing experience of being controlled and empowered by the Holy Spirit. A command, not an option. | 
Submission             | Greek "hupotassō" — to voluntarily place oneself under. A military term for arranging under rank. Voluntary, not forced. | 
One flesh              | The profound union of husband and wife — physical, emotional, spiritual. A picture of Christ's union with His church. | 
Great mystery          | Marriage as a divinely designed picture of Christ's relationship with the church — hidden in Genesis 2, revealed in Ephesians 5. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "Walk in love, as Christ also hath loved us" (v.2). Christ's love was
   sacrificial — He gave Himself. What does sacrificial love look like in
   your daily relationships? Where are you called to give yourself?

2. "Ye were sometimes darkness, but now are ye light" (v.8). You ARE light
   now — not just in the light. How does your life illuminate the darkness
   around you? Where are you still hiding in shadows?

3. "Redeeming the time" (v.16). How are you using your time? Are you making
   the most of opportunities or letting them slip by? What would change if
   you treated every day as a limited, precious resource?

4. "Be filled with the Spirit" (v.18). This is a command — are you obeying
   it? What fills you instead of the Spirit — entertainment, busyness, worry,
   substances? How do you position yourself to be filled?

5. For married men: "Love your wives, even as Christ also loved the church"
   (v.25). Christ's love is sacrificial, sanctifying, nourishing. How are you
   loving your wife in these ways? Where do you fall short of this standard?

6. "This is a great mystery" (v.32). Your marriage (or future marriage) is
   meant to picture the gospel. How does this elevate your view of marriage?
   How does it change how you treat your spouse?

6.notes:
Ephesians - Chapter 6
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Paul addresses household relationships (children/parents, slaves/masters), then commands believers to put on the whole armor of God — belt of truth, breastplate of righteousness, shield of faith, helmet of salvation, and sword of the Spirit — standing firm against spiritual powers.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Paul the Apostle (see Chapter 1 notes for full authorship details).

Historical Context: Chapter 6 concludes Ephesians with the household code
(vv.1-9) and the famous "armor of God" passage (vv.10-20). Paul finishes the
practical section that began in 4:1 by addressing children/parents and
servants/masters, then equips believers for spiritual warfare. Writing from
prison, chained to a Roman soldier, Paul uses the soldier's armor as a
metaphor for spiritual readiness.

Children and Parents (vv.1-4): Children obey parents — "for this is right"
(v.1) and carries a promise of blessing (vv.2-3). Fathers: don't provoke
children to anger but raise them in the Lord's training and instruction (v.4).

Servants and Masters (vv.5-9): Servants work as unto Christ, not men (vv.5-7).
Masters treat servants fairly, knowing they share the same Master in heaven
(v.9). Paul doesn't abolish slavery here but plants seeds that will destroy it
— mutual accountability before God.

The Armor of God (vv.10-20): The Christian life is warfare — not against
people but against spiritual powers (v.12). God provides full armor: belt of
truth, breastplate of righteousness, shoes of the gospel, shield of faith,
helmet of salvation, sword of the Spirit (vv.14-17). Prayer undergirds
everything (v.18).

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/ephesians-6/
  Guzik provides detailed analysis of the household code, spiritual warfare,
  each piece of armor, and the role of prayer in spiritual battle.

Charles Spurgeon:
  Sermon: "The Whole Armour of God" on vv.11-17 (MTP Vol. 11, No. 636)
  "The WHOLE armour. Not part — whole. Leave off one piece and you are
  vulnerable. The soldier who forgets his helmet exposes his head. The one
  who drops his shield catches the fiery darts. God has provided complete
  protection — but you must put it on. It does no good hanging in the
  armory. Put it on — every piece, every day."
  Sermon: "The Sword of the Spirit" on v.17 (MTP Vol. 37, No. 2201)
  "The sword of the Spirit — the Word of God. Notice: it is the only
  offensive weapon in the list. Everything else is defensive — belt, armor,
  shield, helmet, shoes. But the sword strikes. When Jesus faced Satan in
  the wilderness, He used this sword: 'It is written.' Three times the devil
  attacked; three times the Word cut him down. Learn to wield this sword."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "in the Lord"
v.1              | ESV         | Both equivalent. — The sphere of obedience. Children obey parents within the framework of the Lord's authority. If a parent commands something contrary to God, God's authority takes precedence.
v.4              | KJV         | "nurture and admonition"
v.4              | ESV         | "the discipline and instruction" — Greek "paideia kai nouthesia" — training/discipline and instruction/warning. "Paideia" = the whole process of training (includes correction). "Nouthesia" = verbal instruction and warning. Both together = complete parenting.
v.5              | KJV         | "servants"
v.5              | ESV         | Both equivalent. — Greek "douloi" — slaves. Paul addresses the first-century reality of slavery without endorsing it. His principles (mutual accountability before God, v.9) ultimately undermined slavery.
v.6              | KJV         | "eyeservice"
v.6              | ESV         | "not by the way of eye-service" — Greek "ophthalmodoulia" — working only when being watched. A word Paul may have coined. The opposite: working "from the heart" (v.6) as unto Christ.
v.10             | NET         | "Finally, be strengthened in the Lord and in the strength of his might" — "be strengthened" (endynamousthe = be empowered, present passive). WEB: "be strong in the Lord, and in the strength of his might." KJV: "be strong in the Lord." Not self-strength but divine empowerment.
v.11             | KJV         | "wiles"
v.11             | ESV         | "the schemes" — Greek "methodeia" — methods, schemes, strategies. Root of English "method." The devil is strategic, not random. He has specific methods tailored to each person.
v.11             | NET         | "Clothe yourselves with the full armor of God so that you may be able to stand against the schemes of the devil" — "schemes" (methodias = methods/strategies/wiles). WEB: "Put on the whole armor of God." KJV: "Put on the whole armour of God." The enemy has strategies — believers need corresponding equipment.
v.12             | KJV         | "wrestle"
v.12             | ESV         | Both equivalent. — Greek "palē" — hand-to-hand combat, wrestling. Intimate, close-quarters struggle. Not distant warfare but personal engagement with spiritual forces.
v.12             | Multiple    | Four ranks of evil: "principalities" (archai — rulers), "powers" (exousiai — authorities), "rulers of darkness" (kosmokratoras — world-rulers), "spiritual wickedness in high places" (pneumatika ponērias — spiritual forces of evil in the heavenlies).
v.12             | NET         | "For our struggle is not against flesh and blood, but against the rulers, against the powers, against the world rulers of this darkness, against the spiritual forces of evil in the heavens" — four ranks of spiritual opposition. WEB: "against the principalities, against the powers." The real enemy is invisible.
v.16             | KJV         | "fiery darts"
v.16             | ESV         | "the flaming darts" — In ancient warfare, arrows dipped in pitch and set ablaze. The shield (Greek "thureos" — large door-shaped shield) was soaked in water to extinguish them. Faith extinguishes Satan's flaming attacks.
v.17             | KJV         | "sword"
v.17             | ESV         | Both equivalent. — Greek "machaira" — a short sword for close combat, not a long broadsword. Precise, targeted use of Scripture.
v.17             | NET         | "the sword of the Spirit, which is the word of God" — "word" (rhEma = spoken/specific word, not logos = written word). WEB: "the sword of the Spirit, which is the word of God." The only offensive weapon listed — all others are defensive. The spoken word of God is the attack weapon.
v.18             | NET         | "With every prayer and petition, pray at all times in the Spirit" — "at all times" (en panti kairO = in every season/at every opportunity). WEB: "praying at all times in the Spirit." KJV: "Praying always with all prayer." Prayer undergirds all the armor — it is the atmosphere in which the armor is worn.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term                         | Definition                                                   | Notes
-----------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------|------
Nurture and admonition       | The complete parenting approach: training (which includes discipline) and verbal instruction/warning. Both "of the Lord" — centered on God's truth. | 
Eyeservice                   | Working only when being watched. The opposite of working "as unto Christ" with integrity. | 
Whole armour                 | Greek "panoplia" — full armor, complete equipment. Every piece is necessary; none can be omitted. | 
Wiles/Methods                | Satan's strategic schemes tailored to individual weaknesses. He is methodical, not random. | 
Principalities and powers    | Ranks of demonic spiritual beings organized in a hierarchy of evil authority. | 
Belt of truth                | Truth (doctrinal and personal integrity) holds everything together, like a soldier's belt. | 
Breastplate of righteousness | Christ's imputed righteousness and the believer's practical righteousness protecting the heart. | 
Shield of faith              | Active trust in God that extinguishes Satan's flaming attacks of doubt, fear, and temptation. | 
Helmet of salvation          | The assurance of salvation protecting the mind from doubt and despair. | 
Sword of the Spirit          | The Word of God — the only offensive weapon. Used precisely and specifically against the enemy's attacks. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "Provoke not your children to wrath" (v.4). If you're a father, how do
   you balance discipline with encouragement? Are you raising your children
   in the Lord's training, or just in your own frustration?

2. "As unto Christ" (vv.5-7). Whatever your work situation, are you working
   as unto Christ or just for a paycheck? How would your work ethic change if
   you saw Jesus as your true employer?

3. "We wrestle not against flesh and blood" (v.12). When you face conflict,
   do you see the real enemy or just the human in front of you? How does
   recognizing spiritual warfare change how you respond to difficult people?

4. Review each piece of armor (vv.14-17). Which piece are you most likely to
   leave off? Where are you most vulnerable? What specific steps can you take
   to "put on" that piece?

5. "The sword of the Spirit, which is the word of God" (v.17). How well do
   you know Scripture? Can you wield it in the moment of temptation? What
   specific verses do you need to memorize for your specific battles?

6. "Praying always" (v.18). Prayer is the atmosphere in which the armor
   operates. Is prayer a regular discipline or an emergency measure for you?
   How can you grow in "praying always"?
